files.content:
export const html01AccessibleDataTable = (): string => { return `
Quarterly Revenue by Region
Region Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4
North 10,000 12,000 11,500 14,000
South 9,500 11,000 10,200 13,100
East 10,000 12,500 12,400 16,000
Total 29,500 35,500 34,100 43,100
`; };

export const html04ArticleWithStructuredMetadata = (): string => { return ` Designing Reliable Web Interfaces | Example Blog

Designing Reliable Web Interfaces

November 15, 2024

By Avery Chen
42 Market Street, Portland, OR 97205, US

Modern interfaces face unpredictable network conditions, diverse input methods, and varying device capabilities. Reliability starts with semantic structure, sensible defaults, and graceful enhancement.

Use meaningful HTML elements to communicate intent, keep interaction patterns consistent, and prioritize accessibility. Small decisions in markup and content hierarchy often have the biggest impact on usability.

Filed under: Web Development, Accessibility, Frontend Architecture

`; };

export const html07DefinitionListGlossary = (): string => { return `

Technical Glossary

  • API
  • HTTP
  • CSS
  • Cache
  • Refactor
API
A defined set of rules that allows different software systems to communicate with each other.
HTTP
The protocol used for transferring web resources between clients and servers.
CSS
A stylesheet language used to control the presentation and layout of HTML documents.
Cache
A storage layer that keeps frequently accessed data for faster retrieval.
Refactor
The process of restructuring existing code to improve readability or maintainability without changing behavior.
`; };

  'type PaymentEvent = { type: "payment"; amount: number };\n' +

  '\tif (event.type === "user") {\n' +

  '\t}\n' +
  '\treturn `payment:${event.amount}`;\n' +
  '}'
